How to Write Great Unit Tests

By Jane Doe · March 2025

Unit tests are the foundation of a robust software project.

Writing good unit tests takes practice, but the core principles are straightforward.

Keep Tests Small and Focused

Each test should verify exactly one behaviour. If a test fails, you should know immediately what went wrong without reading a wall of assertions.

Use Descriptive Names

A test name like should_return_error_when_input_is_null is far more useful than test1. Treat the test name as the first line of your bug report.

Avoid Shared State

Tests that share mutable state can fail in non-obvious ways depending on execution order. Prefer independent fixtures that are created fresh for every test.

// Good: isolated setup
beforeEach(() => {
  db = new InMemoryDatabase();
});

Following these principles will make your test suite a safety net you can trust.
